per_eval.name: chip-matrix-lookup
per_eval.notes: Fast-path test: with_skill correctly avoided opening any PDF (used chip-matrix.md shortcut); baseline went to datasheet PDFs. Biggest win in this iteration.

per_eval.name: gmac-rgmii-delayline
per_eval.notes: with_skill slower because it (a) read 2 PDFs and (b) wrote a distilled file for future reuse. Baseline went into kernel source (dwmac-rk.c) — quick grep but no reusable knowledge artifact left behind. The trade-off is intentional: skill pays once to amortize across future runs.

per_eval.name: rk356x-usb-gadget-uac
per_eval.notes: Multi-PDF synthesis. with_skill resources-map quickly pointed to all 3 relevant PDFs; baseline burned tokens exploring before converging on the same set.

analyst_observations: Pass rate is saturated (100% both conditions) — the SDK doc base is rich enough that competent agents find correct answers either way. Pass rate is not the discriminator here.

Skill provides clear time/token wins on fast-path queries (eval-1: 53% faster) and multi-PDF synthesis (eval-3: 21% faster). Wins disappear or invert on single-PDF deep dives where baseline can spot-grep the kernel source efficiently (eval-2).

The skill's distinctive value is *cache accumulation*: 2 distilled files were produced in this iteration. The baseline has no equivalent. On a second-run of the same questions, the skill's cost would drop near-zero while baseline would repeat the same exploration.

Assertions are not discriminating because both conditions saturate. For iteration 2, consider adding negative/anti-trigger assertions (e.g. ensuring skill doesn't fire on iMX6ULL prompts) and a 'reuse-from-cache' test that re-runs eval-2 after iteration-1 populated distilled — only with_skill should benefit.

Eval-2 with_skill being slower is the kind of cost we *want* to pay once to gain compounding benefit. Worth flagging in the SKILL.md or workflow doc so future readers understand why a one-PDF dive looks 'inefficient' on a single-shot benchmark.
